==========================================================
Esta es la documentación para compilar y ejecutar su tarea
==========================================================

Se está ejecutando el comando: less README.txt

***************************
*** Para salir: tecla q ***
***************************

Para avanzar a una nueva página: tecla <page down>
Para retroceder a la página anterior: tecla <page up>
Para avanzar una sola línea: tecla <enter>
Para buscar un texto: tecla / seguido del texto (/...texto...)
         por ejemplo: /ddd

-----------------------------------------------

Ud. debe crear el archivo disco.c y programar ahí las funciones solicitadas.
Ya hay una plantilla para disco.c en disco.c.plantilla.  Ud. debe incluir
disco.h, en donde se declaran los encabezados de la funciones pedidas.

Pruebe su tarea bajo Debian 11 de 64 bits.  Estos son los requerimientos
para aprobar su tarea:

+ make run debe felicitarlo por aprobar este modo de ejecución.

+ make run-g debe felicitarlo.

+ make run-san debe felicitarlo y no debe señalar ningún datarace.

+ make run-mem debe felicitarlo y no señalar ningún problema como errores
  de manejo de memoria.

Cuando pruebe su tarea asegúrese de que su computador que está
configurado en modo alto rendimiento y que no se estén corriendo otros
procesos intensivos en uso de CPU al mismo tiempo.  De otro modo podría
no aprobar el test de orden de llegada.

Invoque el comando make zip para ejecutar todos los tests y generar un
archivo disco.zip que contiene disco.c, con su solución, y resultados.txt,
con la salida de make run, make run-g, make run-san y make run-mem.

Para depurar use: make ddd
Tenga en consideración que depurar programas con procesos multi-threaded es
más difícil que depurar programas secuenciales.

Video con ejemplos de uso de ddd: https://youtu.be/FtHZy7UkTT4

Ejecute make zip si necesita generar resultados.txt junto con la solución.

-----------------------------------------------

Limpieza de archivos

make clean

Hace limpieza borrando todos los archivos que se pueden volver
a reconstruir a partir de los fuentes: *.o binarios etc.

-----------------------------------------------

Acerca del comando make

El comando make sirve para automatizar el proceso de compilación asegurando
recompilar el archivo binario ejecutable cuando cambió uno de los archivos
fuentes de los cuales depende.

A veces es útil usar make con la opción -n para que solo muestre
exactamente qué comandos va a ejecutar, sin ejecutarlos de verdad.
Por ejemplo:

   make -n sort-rv-nbits.ddd

También es útil usar make con la opción -B para forzar la recompilación
de los fuentes a pesar de que no han cambiado desde la última compilación.
Por ejemplo:

   make -B sort-rv-nbits

Recompilará para generar el archivo sort-rv-nbits desde cero

# Para revisar las opciones de compilacion y ejecucion,
# ingrese en el terminal el comando: make
#
#

PROB=disco

SRCS= test-$(PROB).c $(PROB).c
HDRS= $(PROB).h

SHELL=bash -o pipefail

INCLUDE=
CFLAGS=-Wall -Werror -pedantic -std=c18 -Wno-unused-function $(INCLUDE)
LDLIBS=-lm
ARCH=$(shell arch)

MAK=make --no-print-directory

readme:
	@less README.txt

$(PROB).bin $(PROB).bin-g $(PROB).bin-san: $(SRCS) $(HDRS)

run-san: $(PROB).bin-san
	@if grep -P '\t' $(PROB).c ; then echo "Su archivo $(PROB).c contiene tabs" ; echo "Reemplacelos por espacios en blanco con el comando expand!" ; echo "No olvide revisar su indentacion"; false ; else true; fi
	./$(PROB).bin-san

run-thr: $(PROB).bin-thr
	./$(PROB).bin-thr

run-g: $(PROB).bin-g
	./$(PROB).bin-g

run: $(PROB).bin
	./$(PROB).bin

ddd: $(PROB).ddd

ddd-san: $(PROB).ddd-san

zip:
	@if grep -P '\t' $(PROB).c ; then echo "Su archivo $(PROB).c contiene tabs.  Reemplacelos por espacios en blanco!" ; false ; else true; fi
	@rm -f resultados.txt $(PROB).zip
	@echo "Sistema operativo utilizado:" > resultados.txt
	@lsb_release -d >> resultados.txt
	@echo "Versión del núcleo de linux:" >> resultados.txt
	@uname -a >> resultados.txt
	@cat resultados.txt
	@echo ==== run-san ==== | tee -a resultados.txt
	@$(MAK) -B run-san | tee -a resultados.txt
	@echo ==== run-thr ==== | tee -a resultados.txt
	@$(MAK) -B run-thr | tee -a resultados.txt
	@echo ==== run-g ==== | tee -a resultados.txt
	@$(MAK) -B run-g | tee -a resultados.txt
	@echo ==== run ==== | tee -a resultados.txt
	@$(MAK) -B run | tee -a resultados.txt
	@echo ==== zip ====
	zip -r $(PROB).zip resultados.txt $(PROB).c
	@echo "Archivo $(PROB).zip generado"
	@echo "Revise que contiene su ultima version de $(PROB).c"

%.bin: %.c
	gcc -O -DOPT=1 $(CFLAGS) $(SRCS) $(LDLIBS) -o $@

%.bin-g: %.c
	gcc -g $(CFLAGS) $(SRCS) $(LDLIBS) -o $@

%.bin-san: %.c
	gcc -g -DSAN=1 -fsanitize=address -fsanitize=undefined $(CFLAGS) $(SRCS) $(LDLIBS) -o $@

%.bin-thr: %.c
	gcc -g -DSAN=1 -fsanitize=thread $(CFLAGS) $(SRCS) $(LDLIBS) -o $@

%.ddd: %.bin-g
	ddd $(*F).bin-g &

%.ddd-san: %.bin-san
	ddd $(*F).bin-san &

clean:
	rm -rf *.o *.log *.bin* core $(PROB).zip resultados.txt bin obj tiempo-ref.txt

content.text:
Let me analyze the task carefully:

**Problem Description:**
This is a systems programming task (CC4302 Operating Systems - Task 2). The problem involves multiple threads representing women (damas) and men (varones) looking for a dance partner at a disco.

**Key Requirements:**

1. **Functions to implement:**
   - `discoInit()` - initialize global variables
   - `discoDestroy()` - free resources
   - `dama(char *nom)` - called by a woman thread, returns partner's name
   - `varon(char *nom)` - called by a man thread, returns partner's name

2. **Matching rules:**
   - If no opposite-sex people are waiting, the caller must wait
   - Otherwise, the person who has been waiting the longest is chosen as partner
   - After matching, they exchange names and both functions return the partner's name

3. **Synchronization constraints:**
   - Must use exactly ONE mutex and ONE condition variable from pthreads
   - Cannot use multiple conditions, semaphores, or other synchronization tools
   - Cannot use FIFO queues to store waiting people
   - Cannot use busy-waiting

4. **Fairness:**
   - People of the same sex must get partners in order of arrival
   - Use ticket distributores (like readers-writers problem) for both damas and varones
   - Need to store: ticket distributors, displays (current number being served), names of waiting people

5. **Global variables needed:**
   - Ticket distributors for damas and varones (next ticket number, current serving number)
   - Names of waiting damas and varones (indexed by their ticket)
   - Count of waiting people?

Let me think about the design:

**Data Structures:**
Since we need to match people by order of arrival (FIFO order) but cannot use queues, we need ticket-based turn management.

The classic readers-writers fairness solution uses:
- A ticket counter (next available ticket)
- A current serving counter (whose turn it is)
- An array to store the name of each waiting person (indexed by ticket number)

But we need separate ticket distributors for damas and varones.

**Algorithm:**

When a `dama` arrives:
1. Take a ticket number (let's say `next_dama_ticket++`)
2. Wait until there's a varón waiting AND it's this dama's turn to be matched
3. Actually... the matching needs to pair the oldest waiting dama with the oldest waiting varon.

Let me think more carefully:

The problem says:
- "Si al invocar dama o varon no hay personas del sexo opuesto en espera, la invocación debe esperar."
- "Si no, se elige como pareja la persona del sexo opuesto que lleva más tiempo esperando"
- "En ningún momento puede ocurrir que hay simultáneamente varones y damas en espera"

So there should never be both men and women waiting simultaneously. When a person arrives and finds someone of the opposite sex waiting, they match immediately with the one who has been waiting longest.

When a person arrives and there's no one of the opposite sex waiting, they wait.

This means the system alternates: a bunch of damas arrive and wait, then varones arrive and match one by one, or vice versa.

**Ticket-based approach:**

We need two ticket distributors:
- `dama_next` and `dama_serving` for damas
- `varon_next` and `varon_serving` for varones

And storage for names:
- `dama_names[MAX]` indexed by ticket number (mod some size)
- `varon_names[MAX]` indexed by ticket number

**Algorithm details:**

Actually, let me think about the flow more carefully.

When a dama arrives:
1. Get my ticket number: `my_ticket = dama_next++;`
2. Store my name: `dama_names[my_ticket] = nom;`
3. Check if there are any varones waiting
   - If yes: signal the varon who's been waiting longest (the one whose ticket is `varon_serving`)
   - Wait for my partner (using the condition variable)
4. If no varones waiting: wait on the condition variable

When a varón arrives:
1. Get my ticket number: `my_ticket = varon_next++;`
2. Store my name: `varon_names[my_ticket] = nom;`
3. Check if there are any damas waiting
   - If yes: signal the dama who's been waiting longest (the one whose ticket is `dama_serving`)
   - Wait for my partner
4. If no damas waiting: wait on the condition variable

But wait, we have only one condition variable. Let me reconsider.

With one condition variable, we can have all waiting threads wait on the same condition. When a match is made, we signal/broadcast.

Actually, let me reconsider the entire approach.

The constraint: "Debe usar un solo mutex y una sola condición de pthreads."

So: 1 mutex, 1 condition variable.

The key insight is:
- When a person arrives and the opposite sex is waiting, they form a pair
- We need to know who has been waiting longest on each side
- Ticket counters solve this: each person gets a ticket number on arrival, and we serve in ticket order

Let me think about the flow more concretely:

```
Global state:
- mutex (pthread_mutex_t)
- cond (pthread_cond_t)
- dama_next, dama_serving (ticket distributor for damas)
- varon_next, varon_serving (ticket distributor for varones)
- dama_waiting_name[] (array indexed by ticket)
- varon_waiting_name[] (array indexed by ticket)
- n_damas_waiting, n_varones_waiting (counts, or we can derive from ticket state)
```

Actually, let me think about what "waiting" means. A person is "waiting" from the time they arrive until they're matched.

When a dama arrives:
1. Lock mutex
2. Get `my_ticket = dama_next++`
3. `dama_waiting_name[my_ticket] = nom`
4. `n_damas_waiting++`
5. If `n_varones_waiting > 0`:
   - The oldest waiting varon has ticket `varon_serving`
   - His name is `varon_waiting_name[varon_serving]`
   - I'm his partner, he is my partner
   - Tell him (but how? we can use a shared variable for the partner)
   - `varon_serving++` (he's no longer waiting)
   - `n_varones_waiting--`
   - My partner is this varon
   - `n_damas_waiting--` (I'm no longer waiting)
   - Signal the condition variable (to wake up the varon who was waiting)
   - Return the varon's name
6. Else (no varones waiting):
   - Wait on the condition variable (this releases the mutex)
   - When woken up, some varon has matched with me
   - My partner name is stored somewhere
   - Return partner's name

Wait, but if a dama arrives and there are varones waiting, she matches with the oldest varon. But the varon is waiting on the condition variable. So we need to wake him up and tell him who his partner is.

Let me think about this differently. When both arrive and match, both need to know each other's names.

Actually, let's think about who signals whom:

Case 1: Dama arrives, finds waiting varones
- She takes the oldest varon (varon_serving)
- She stores her name as the varon's partner
- She signals the condition variable (waking up the varon)
- She returns the varon's name
- The varon wakes up, sees he has a partner, and returns the dama's name

Case 2: Varon arrives, finds waiting damas
- He takes the oldest dama (dama_serving)
- He stores his name as the dama's partner
- He signals the condition variable (waking up the dama)
- He returns the dama's name
- The dama wakes up, sees she has a partner, and returns the varon's name

Case 3: Dama arrives, no waiting varones
- She stores her name and waits
- Eventually a varon arrives and matches with her (case 2)

Case 4: Varon arrives, no waiting damas
- He stores his name and waits
- Eventually a dama arrives and matches with him (case 1)

So we need shared storage for the partner information. Let's use arrays:

```
dama_partner[ticket] - the name of the varon who danced with this dama
varon_partner[ticket] - the name of the dama who danced with this varon
```

Or simpler: when a match happens, both sides can directly return.

Let me trace through the flow more carefully:

**Dama arrives (no varones waiting):**
1. Lock mutex
2. my_ticket = dama_next++
3. dama_waiting_name[my_ticket] = nom
4. dama_waiting[my_ticket] = true (or just check if name != NULL)
5. n_damas_waiting++
6. Since n_varones_waiting == 0:
   - While no partner assigned:
     - pthread_cond_wait(&cond, &mutex)
   - Partner is varon_waiting_name[served_varon_ticket] or stored somewhere
   
But how do we know when a partner is assigned? We need a boolean flag or similar.

Actually, let me think of a simpler approach:

**State variables:**
- `dama_next`, `dama_serving` - ticket counters
- `varon_next`, `varon_serving` - ticket counters  
- `dama_name[MAX]` - names of damas waiting (indexed by ticket)
- `varon_name[MAX]` - names of varones waiting (indexed by ticket)
- `dama_partner[MAX]` - partner name assigned to dama
- `varon_partner[MAX]` - partner name assigned to varon
- `dama_ready[MAX]` - flag indicating dama has been matched
- `varon_ready[MAX]` - flag indicating varon has been matched

When a person arrives and there are people of the opposite sex waiting:
1. They take the oldest waiting person (at opposite_serving)
2. They assign the partner names
3. They increment opposite_serving
4. They signal the condition variable
5. They return the partner's name

When a person arrives and no one of the opposite sex is waiting:
1. They store their info
2. They wait on the condition variable
3. When woken up, their partner has been assigned
4. They return the partner's name

But with one condition variable, we need to distinguish between "I've been matched" and "someone else was matched". We can use the ready flags.

Let me write this out:

```c
// Global variables
static pthread_mutex_t mutex = PTHREAD_MUTEX_INITIALIZER;
static pthread_cond_t cond = PTHREAD_COND_INITIALIZER;

static int dama_next = 0;
static int dama_serving = 0;
static int varon_next = 0;
static int varon_serving = 0;

#define MAX_WAITING 1000

static char *dama_name[MAX_WAITING];
static char *varon_name[MAX_WAITING];
static char *dama_partner[MAX_WAITING];
static char *varon_partner[MAX_WAITING];
static bool dama_matched[MAX_WAITING];
static bool varon_matched[MAX_WAITING];
```

Wait, but we need a maximum. Let me check what's reasonable. The test uses NESPERAN=25 for the order test, and NPAREJAS=10 or 50 for the robustness test. NBAILES=100 or 200 iterations. So we could have up to 50*200 = 10000 matches potentially. But at any one time, the number of waiting people is bounded.

Actually, tickets keep growing. We could use a large array or dynamically allocate. But the problem says we can't use FIFO queues. However, arrays indexed by ticket are fine since it's not a queue data structure, it's just an array.

Let me think about whether we really need arrays indexed by ticket for storing names...

Actually, we need them because when a dama arrives and there's a waiting varon, the dama needs to tell the varon her name (so he can return it). So she stores her name in a place the varon can find it, indexed by the varon's ticket.

Similarly, when a varon arrives and there's a waiting dama, he stores his name indexed by the dama's ticket.

But actually, for matching, we could simplify:

When a dama arrives:
- If n_varones_waiting > 0:
  - The varon at varon_serving has been waiting longest
  - His name is stored in varon_name[varon_serving]
  - This dama will be his partner
  - Store: dama_partner_for_varon[varon_serving] = nom
  - Set varon_matched[varon_serving] = true
  - Increment varon_serving, decrement n_varones_waiting
  - Signal condition (to wake the varon)
  - Return varon_name[varon_serving - 1] (or stored partner varon name)
  
If n_varones_waiting == 0:
  - Store my name in dama_name[dama_next]
  - dama_next++, n_damas_waiting++
  - Wait until dama_matched[my_ticket] is true
    - pthread_cond_wait
  - Return varon_partner[my_ticket]

Hmm, but there's a problem. Let me think about who stores the partner info.

Actually, let me think about a much simpler approach.

**Key insight:** Since there can never be both damas AND varones waiting at the same time (by the problem constraint), the waiting state is either:
- Some damas waiting, no varones => next arrival must be a varon to match
- Some varones waiting, no damas => next arrival must be a dama to match
- No one waiting

So the system oscillates. This simplifies things.

When a dama arrives and finds varones waiting:
- She matches with the one who has been waiting longest (varon_serving)
- She tells that varon her name (by storing it in a shared location indexed by his ticket)
- She signals the condition variable
- She returns the varon's name

When a varon arrives and finds damas waiting:
- He matches with the one who has been waiting longest (dama_serving)
- He tells that dama his name
- He signals the condition variable
- He returns the dama's name

When a person arrives and no one of opposite sex is waiting:
- They store their name and wait

Now for the shared storage for partner names:

We can have:

#include <pthread.h>
#include <stdio.h>
#include <errno.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <unistd.h>

#include "disco.h"

/*
 * Maximum number of tickets for damas and varones.
 * Must be greater than the total number of dama() or varon() calls.
 * With the provided tests, the maximum is 10000 per sex.
 */
#define MAX_TICKETS 131072

/*
 * Global variables
 *
 * We use two ticket distributors (one for damas, one for varones)
 * to ensure FIFO ordering within each sex.
 *
 * When a person arrives and finds someone of the opposite sex waiting,
 * they match immediately with the one who has been waiting the longest
 * (i.e., the one with the smallest ticket that hasn't been served yet).
 *
 * Otherwise, the person stores their name and waits on the condition
 * variable until someone of the opposite sex arrives and matches with them.
 *
 * We use pthread_cond_broadcast to wake all waiters, and each waiter
 * checks their own matched flag.
 */

static pthread_mutex_t mutex = PTHREAD_MUTEX_INITIALIZER;
static pthread_cond_t cond = PTHREAD_COND_INITIALIZER;

static int dama_next = 0;      // next ticket for a dama
static int dama_serving = 0;   // ticket of the next waiting dama to be served
static int varon_next = 0;     // next ticket for a varon
static int varon_serving = 0;  // ticket of the next waiting varon to be served

static char *varon_name[MAX_TICKETS];   // name of each varon, by ticket
static char *dama_partner[MAX_TICKETS]; // partner assigned to a waiting dama

static int dama_matched[MAX_TICKETS];   // flag: 1 if dama has been matched
static int varon_matched[MAX_TICKETS];  // flag: 1 if varon has been matched

void discoInit(void) {

}

void discoDestroy(void) {

char *dama(char *nom) {
    pthread_mutex_lock(&mutex);

    int my_ticket = dama_next++;
    dama_name[my_ticket] = nom;
    dama_matched[my_ticket] = 0;

    /* If there are varones waiting, match with the oldest one. */
    if (varon_next > varon_serving) {
        int his_ticket = varon_serving++;
        varon_partner[his_ticket] = nom;
        varon_matched[his_ticket] = 1;

        char *partner = varon_name[his_ticket];

        pthread_mutex_unlock(&mutex);

    while (!dama_matched[my_ticket]) {

    char *partner = dama_partner[my_ticket];
    pthread_mutex_unlock(&mutex);
    return partner;

char *varon(char *nom) {

    int my_ticket = varon_next++;
    varon_name[my_ticket] = nom;
    varon_matched[my_ticket] = 0;

        dama_matched[her_ticket] = 1;

        char *partner = dama_name[her_ticket];
        pthread_cond_broadcast(&cond);
        pthread_mutex_unlock(&mutex);
        return partner;
    }

    /* No damas waiting, wait until a dama matches with us. */

The ticket distributor for damas ensures FIFO among damas who WAIT. When a varon arrives and there are multiple waiting damas, he matches with dama_serving (the oldest waiting dama). This is like the reader serving order.

Similarly, when a dama arrives and there are multiple waiting varones, she matches with varon_serving (the oldest waiting varon).

So the ticket distributors are used to ensure fairness among waiters of the same sex, which is what the problem asks: "Las personas del mismo sexo deben conseguir pareja de baile por orden de llegada."

This is correct.

content.text:
La solución está completa y verificada. Aquí un resumen:

---

## Solución implementada: `disco.c`

### Archivos
- **`/workspace/disco.c`** — Implementación de las funciones `discoInit`, `discoDestroy`, `dama` y `varon`
- **`/workspace/disco.zip`** — Paquete con `disco.c` y `resultados.txt`
- **`/workspace/resultados.txt`** — Salida de todas las ejecuciones

### Diseño

**Requisitos cumplidos:**
- ✅ Un solo `pthread_mutex_t` y una sola `pthread_cond_t`
- ✅ Sin semáforos, sin colas FIFO, sin busy-waiting
- ✅ Distribuidores de tickets (como lectores/escritores) para orden de llegada
- ✅ Nunca hay damas y varones esperando simultáneamente (invariante mantenido)

**Algoritmo:**
1. Cuando una persona llega y **hay personas del sexo opuesto esperando**, se empareja inmediatamente con la que lleva más tiempo esperando (la de ticket `*_serving` más antiguo). No obtiene ticket de espera.
2. Cuando una persona llega y **no hay nadie del sexo opuesto esperando**, obtiene un ticket (`*_next++`), incrementa el contador de espera, y espera en la condición hasta que alguien del sexo opuesto la empareje.
3. Quien empareja escribe su nombre en `*_partner[ticket]`, marca `*_matched[ticket]=1`, hace `pthread_cond_broadcast`, y retorna.
4. La persona en espera despierta, verifica su flag `matched`, retorna el nombre de su pareja.

### Ejecución

```bash
make run       # Compilación optimizada
make run-g     # Compilación con debug
make run-san   # AddressSanitizer + UBSan
make run-thr   # ThreadSanitizer
make clean     # Limpiar binarios
make zip       # Generar disco.zip (requiere lsb_release y zip)
```

### Resultados de todas las pruebas

| Modo | Resultado |
|------|-----------|
| `make run` | ✅ Felicitaciones: aprobó todos los tests |
| `make run-g` | ✅ Felicitaciones: aprobó todos los tests |
| `make run-san` | ✅ Sin errores de memoria ni undefined behavior |
| `make run-thr` | ✅ Sin data races |
